# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for a Node.js Express application. Use the official Node.js base image and install dependencies with npm install. The app listens on port 3000. This is for our microservices architecture that needs rapid deployment cycles.
# Category: container_security

# Docker File to Build a NodeJs Express Application in an Ubuntu Environment With NPM Install Dependencies and Listening at Port 3000
FROM node:14 # Use official Node image with npm already installed (upstream) as base. Using this will ensure all dependencies are available during build time for security purposes, hence avoiding manual work to install them via apt/yum or similar tooling on each runtime instance. It's recommended also use the LTS version of node according latest updates and patches
WORKDIR /usr/src/app  # Changes default directory into WORKDIR path inside container which will be "/user/srv" in ubuntu environment by DEFAULT, but we want to set it elsewhere for project root. This way is safer as our app's code may reside anywhere and can run without modifications
COPY package*.json ./  # copy the dependencies list file created from npm install into docker image context ("/usr/src/app")
RUN npm install   # Install all dependecies listed in "package.json" via NPM INSTALL, this runs on a new container for each change to Dockerfile because of cache usage - you can use the cached dependencies and then run `npm i` later if needed (to update your package list after being modified)
COPY . ./  # Copy all files into docker context where npm install is executed. By copying over whole project, it means that changes made to src folder will reflect in Docker image itself due copy operation of source file on host machine and destination path inside container are the same location by default but can be changed using relative paths or absolute Paths if required
EXPOSE 3000 # Expose port which application listens from (Node.js app) to communicate with outside world, Docker uses this information during runtime for creating a network service in host and then connect back into container's IP address on defined PORT of NodeJS App when running the Container
CMD ["node", "app.js"] # Set default command run by docker if no other is specified so node app runs from current directory with specific file (By Default it will be `app.js`) in production environment for using Dockerfile, use CMD instruction to specify more details about your application's entry point(i.e., Node js server script).
